mappings.rationaleAI: X-ray crystallography's role in revealing atomic arrangements in crystals, diffraction techniques pioneered by the Braggs, and structural determination across physics, chemistry, and biology directly address crystal patterns (A.1), X-ray diffraction by crystal structures (A.2), and crystal growth and preparation (A.3).

Zero electrical resistance discovered by Kamerlingh Onnes in 1911, BCS theory of Cooper pairs, the Meissner effect expelling magnetic fields, transition temperatures, energy gaps, and high-temperature superconductors directly address superconductivity in metals (D.8), thermal properties of superconductors (D.8.a), and magnetic/electromagnetic properties including the Meissner effect and phase coherence (D.8.b). The quantum mechanical explanation connects to energy-band theory (B.5.d).

The glass transition from molten liquid to amorphous solid, the absence of ordered molecular structure distinguishing glass from crystalline solids, and modern innovations in float glass and metallic glasses directly address the glassy state (F), temperature and composition effects on glass properties (F.1), glass structure (F.2), and the classification of solids by bonding and structure (B.1).

Electrical conduction based on atomic structure, semiconductor physics enabling transistors, solar cells, and silicon chips, and the distinction between conductors, semiconductors, and superconductors address semiconductor properties (E.1), conduction mechanisms (E.2), electrical conduction in semiconductors (E.3), semiconductor applications (E.4), the free electron model for metals (D.2), and electron mobility in conductors, insulators, and semiconductors (B.5.a).

Classification of solid, liquid, gas, and plasma by molecular arrangement and intermolecular forces, with specific discussion of glasses as disordered solids and liquid crystals exhibiting directional molecular order, addresses the classification of solids by electronic structure and bonding (B.1) and the glassy/amorphous state including glass structure (F, F.2).

The exclusion principle governing electron shell configurations, chemical bonding, and condensed matter behavior underpins the electronic structure of solids (B.5), electron behavior in conductors, insulators, and semiconductors (B.5.a), energy-band theory (B.5.d), and the electronic structure of metals including conduction electron interactions with periodic lattices (D.3).

Einstein's photoelectric effect explanation, wave-particle duality, and applications in photovoltaic cells, LEDs, and lasers directly address the interaction of light with solids (B.7), reflection, absorption, and transmission of photons (B.7.a), electromagnetic radiation generation from solids (B.7.b), the photoelectric effect (B.7.c, B.5.b), and optical effects in semiconductors including the photovoltaic effect and luminescence (E.4.a).

Thomson's discovery of the electron, its role in probing atomic structure, and connections to condensed matter physics are foundational to the electronic structure of solids (B.5), electron mobility in conductors and insulators (B.5.a), the free electron model for metal conductivity (D.2), conduction electron behavior in metallic lattices (D.3), and mechanisms of conduction in semiconductors (E.2).

Group theory formalization of symmetry and its direct application to crystallography and molecular structure address symmetry considerations in crystal system classification (A.1.b) and atomic patterns in crystals (A.1).

Ice exhibiting at least fifteen distinct crystalline forms under varying pressures exemplifies crystal polymorphism and the relationship between atomic arrangement and macroscopic properties, addressing crystal patterns (A.1) and classification of solids including hydrogen-bonded solids (B.1).

Pasteur's foundational work demonstrating left and right-handed crystal forms of tartrate molecules established key principles of crystallographic symmetry (A.1.b) and the relationship between molecular chirality and crystal structure (A.1).
